hypothesis: For hospital readmission prediction, a sequence model trained on temporally ordered EHR events from the prior 30 days will outperform a baseline using only aggregated summary features in predicting 30-day unplanned readmission risk.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML using longitudinal electronic health records from an acute-care hospital system
available_data: Realistic data would include inpatient and outpatient encounter histories, diagnoses, procedures, medications, lab results, vitals, and discharge summaries collected over multiple years from a hospital EHR. Labels would be available from admissions records indicating whether each discharge was followed by an unplanned readmission within 30 days, with enough volume to support tens of thousands of index discharges and strong temporal drift across years and departments.
success_metric_context: Clinicians and care managers care about identifying high-risk discharges early enough to allocate follow-up resources, reduce avoidable readmissions, and improve patient outcomes. Better performance means higher discrimination and better calibrated risk scores at clinically actionable thresholds, not just higher overall accuracy.
design_challenges: The data are temporally structured, so the evaluation must use chronological splits to avoid leakage from future practice patterns, coding changes, or patient overlap across train and test periods.

Readmission labels are affected by competing factors such as planned follow-up admissions, differing discharge practices across services, and censoring when patients seek care outside the health system.
known_confounds: Any improvement could be driven by the sequence model capturing recency or utilization intensity rather than true clinical state, so a critic would need to check whether gains persist after controlling for encounter frequency, length of stay, and discharge service.

hypothesis: In e-commerce product search, a learning-to-rank model that incorporates query-product semantic embeddings and recent click feedback will outperform a BM25-only retrieval baseline in top-10 result relevance, especially for long-tail and synonym-heavy queries.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search relevance for a large retail platform
available_data: The platform would realistically have query logs, product catalog text, click-through and add-to-cart events, and manually judged relevance labels for a sample of query-product pairs. Data could span millions of searches over several months, with labels available for a smaller curated set used in offline evaluation.
success_metric_context: The business goal is to show more relevant products earlier in search results, improving click-through rate, add-to-cart rate, and downstream conversion. 'Better' means users find suitable items faster, with fewer reformulations and abandoned searches.
design_challenges: Search data is highly position-biased, so an evaluation must account for implicit feedback not being a direct relevance signal.
The query distribution is long-tailed and seasonal, so the experiment must handle rare terms, catalog churn, and temporal leakage from recent clicks or inventory changes.
known_confounds: Any improvement could be caused by freshness effects, popularity priors, or better handling of head queries rather than true semantic relevance gains.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model that jointly encodes a patient's longitudinal EHR sequence and prior medication fill history will predict 30-day hospital readmission better than a baseline logistic regression model using only the latest-visit structured features, for adult general-medicine discharges.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — inpatient readmission prediction from electronic health records and pharmacy claims
available_data: Hospitals and integrated delivery networks routinely store encounter-level EHR data including demographics, diagnoses, procedures, lab results, vitals, discharge disposition, and medication orders, while pharmacy claims provide outpatient fill history after discharge. A realistic study could use 2–5 years of retrospective adult inpatient discharges from one or more health systems, with the label defined as any unplanned readmission within 30 days. The data would be moderately large, with tens to hundreds of thousands of index discharges and a strong class imbalance because readmission is relatively infrequent.
success_metric_context: Operationally, better performance means identifying high-risk patients soon after discharge so care managers can target follow-up calls, medication reconciliation, and early clinic appointments. The metric should reflect both discrimination and practical workload constraints, since hospitals care about how many true readmissions can be captured within a limited outreach budget.
design_challenges: The data have strong temporal structure, so a chronological split is needed to avoid leakage from future practice patterns and seasonality in admission rates.

Medication fill history is often missing or delayed for patients using outside pharmacies, which can create differential missingness and make the learned benefit of sequence modeling hard to interpret.
known_confounds: Any lift over the baseline could be driven by access to richer patient history, better handling of missingness, or temporal leakage from post-discharge information rather than the transformer architecture itself.

hypothesis: A survival model that uses longitudinal EHR events plus baseline comorbidity history will predict 90-day unplanned readmission after hospital discharge more accurately than a static admission-time logistic regression baseline, especially for patients with highly irregular prior utilization patterns.
domain: Hospital medicine using inpatient electronic health records from a multi-hospital health system
available_data: The data would realistically come from inpatient EHRs, including admission/discharge timestamps, diagnoses, procedures, medications, lab results, prior encounters, and discharge disposition for hundreds of thousands of hospitalizations over several years. Labels are available from observed return admissions within 90 days, with censoring from death or loss to follow-up handled through the health system’s records and linked claims where available.
success_metric_context: Operationally, hospitals care about identifying patients at high risk of avoidable readmission so they can target transitional care resources, follow-up appointments, and medication reconciliation. Better performance means improved ranking and calibration of near-term readmission risk, not just higher AUC, because intervention capacity is limited and the top-risk patients matter most.
design_challenges: The data are temporally structured and irregularly sampled, so the experiment must decide how to represent event histories without leaking post-discharge information and must use chronological splitting to avoid train-test contamination.

Readmission risk is heavily confounded by discharge practices, length of stay, and competing risk of death, so the evaluation must distinguish true predictive gain from artifacts of care intensity and censoring.
known_confounds: Observed improvements could be driven by simple proxies such as length of stay, recent utilization frequency, or hospital/unit-specific discharge behavior rather than the longitudinal modeling approach itself.

hypothesis: In a retail logistics setting, a gradient-boosted model trained on hourly warehouse scan counts, courier route status, and weather features will forecast next-day parcel delay volume more accurately than a model using only historical delay totals, reducing forecast error on peak-demand periods.
domain: Transportation / logistics — last-mile parcel delivery operations for a regional courier network
available_data: Operational data would realistically include hourly parcel intake, sortation scans, route assignments, dispatch times, exception codes, and local weather observations from one or more distribution hubs. Labels would be next-day delay counts or delay minutes, available over at least 12-24 months to capture weekly seasonality, holidays, and peak shopping periods.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about staffing, vehicle allocation, and rerouting decisions, so better means more accurate forecasts of delay volume during high-volume days when planning errors are costly. Performance should matter most when delays spike, not just on average low-variance days.
design_challenges: The series has strong calendar effects and abrupt regime changes around holidays, promotions, and weather events, so evaluation should avoid random splits and preserve chronological ordering.
Delay volume is likely zero-inflated and heavy-tailed, which makes metric choice non-trivial because average error may hide poor performance on rare but operationally important disruption days.
known_confounds: Any observed improvement could be due to leakage from near-real-time operational variables that are themselves downstream of delays, so a careful critic would verify feature timestamps and availability at prediction time.

hypothesis: For first-time e-commerce buyers, a session-level sequential model trained on clickstream events and product-view dwell times will predict 30-day purchase conversion better than a static feature baseline built only from aggregated user, device, and referral attributes.
domain: E-commerce marketplace user acquisition and conversion optimization
available_data: A large marketplace or retail platform would typically have anonymized session logs with page views, searches, clicks, cart events, dwell times, device type, traffic source, and eventual purchase labels within a 30-day attribution window. The data would span many millions of sessions over several months, with sparse positive labels because only a small fraction of first-time visits convert.
success_metric_context: The business cares about identifying high-intent new visitors early enough to trigger interventions such as personalized offers, onboarding prompts, or live chat. Better means improved ranking/calibration of conversion likelihood so marketing and product teams can target limited incentives more efficiently without increasing unnecessary discounting.
design_challenges: The data has strong temporal dependence within sessions, so the evaluation must respect session order and avoid leakage from events occurring after the prediction point.

Conversion is typically highly imbalanced and seasonally shifted by campaigns or holidays, so the experiment must handle class imbalance and temporally separated validation to ensure gains are not just due to campaign-specific patterns.
known_confounds: Any apparent lift could be caused by the sequential model capturing session length or traffic-source artifacts rather than genuine intent signals, so a critic would check matched baselines and performance within key acquisition channels.

hypothesis: For hospital sepsis prediction, a temporally aware model that uses irregularly sampled vital-sign trajectories and recent lab trends will predict 6-hour sepsis onset more accurately than a static feature baseline built from the latest-charted values and summary statistics, as measured by out-of-time AUROC and early-warning recall.
domain: Clinical acute care: ICU and emergency department sepsis risk prediction from electronic health records
available_data: Large hospitals routinely store time-stamped vitals, lab results, medication administrations, diagnoses, and clinician orders in EHR systems, with sepsis labels derivable from charted onset criteria or billing/clinical adjudication. A realistic study could use several years of ICU and ED encounters, with tens of thousands of patient stays and labels available retrospectively after discharge. The data are inherently irregular, multivariate, and partially missing, with outcomes linked to events occurring hours before diagnosis.
success_metric_context: Clinically, the goal is to identify deteriorating patients early enough to trigger intervention without overwhelming staff with false alarms. Better performance means not just higher discrimination, but more true warnings in the actionable window before sepsis onset while keeping alert volume manageable.
design_challenges: The temporal nature of the signals requires a chronological split to avoid leakage from future information and to evaluate whether the model generalizes across changing practice patterns.

Class imbalance and label timing are non-trivial because sepsis onset is noisy and clinically delayed, so the experiment must define a fair prediction horizon and avoid crediting models that only detect already-declared sepsis.
known_confounds: Apparent gains could come from richer missingness patterns, site-specific documentation habits, or label-definition artifacts rather than genuine temporal modeling improvements, so a critic would check calibration, subgroup stability, and performance under alternate sepsis definitions.

hypothesis: For hospital readmission prediction, a transformer model trained on longitudinal EHR sequences (diagnoses, medications, labs, and encounter timing) will outperform a static tabular baseline built from the same data aggregated over the index admission in AUPRC for 30-day unplanned readmission.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — inpatient electronic health records from a multi-hospital health system
available_data: A large health system would realistically have 3–5 years of inpatient EHR data, including encounter histories, coded diagnoses/procedures, medication administrations, laboratory results, vital signs, and discharge outcomes for tens to hundreds of thousands of admissions. The label is available from administrative follow-up data: whether a patient had an unplanned readmission within 30 days of discharge. Temporal ordering, missingness patterns, and varying lengths of prior history would all be present in the raw data.
success_metric_context: Clinicians and care managers care about identifying patients at highest risk of avoidable readmission so they can target discharge planning and post-discharge outreach. Because readmissions are relatively rare, improvement should be judged primarily by precision-recall behavior and top-risk capture, not just overall accuracy.
design_challenges: The data have strong temporal structure and leakage risks, so the evaluation must respect chronology and ensure that only information available before discharge is used.
Readmission risk is highly imbalanced and varies by service line and hospital, so the experiment must handle class imbalance and potential site-specific distribution shift when comparing models.
known_confounds: Any performance gain could come from better handling of missingness, note/record length, or hospital-specific coding practices rather than true sequence modeling of patient trajectory.

hypothesis: For a marketplace search ranking task on an e-commerce platform, a learning-to-rank model that incorporates query-to-product semantic embeddings and recent click-through feedback will rank relevant products higher than a baseline ranker using only lexical BM25 and handcrafted popularity features, as measured by offline NDCG on held-out search sessions.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search ranking for consumer product discovery
available_data: A large e-commerce platform would typically have search logs containing user queries, product impressions, clicks, add-to-cart events, and purchases over several months or years, along with product catalog metadata such as titles, descriptions, categories, prices, and inventory status. Label signals can be derived from implicit feedback at the session or impression level, with enough volume to support training and time-based evaluation across changing catalog and query trends.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about helping shoppers find relevant items faster, which should translate into higher click-through, add-to-cart, and purchase rates while reducing reformulations and abandonment. Better model performance means ranking results that align with user intent and drive downstream conversion, not merely improving offline similarity scores.
design_challenges: Search behavior is temporally non-stationary because seasonal trends, promotions, and catalog changes shift query intent and product availability, so evaluation must use chronological splits rather than random splits.

Implicit feedback is biased by position and exposure, so apparent gains can be confounded by click bias and popularity effects unless the evaluation and labeling strategy account for examination bias.
known_confounds: Observed improvements could be explained by exposure bias, popularity priors, or better handling of frequently searched branded queries rather than genuine semantic relevance gains.

hypothesis: For hospital-acquired sepsis prediction, a model that combines structured EHR time-series features with transformer embeddings from free-text nursing notes will outperform a structured-only gradient-boosted baseline in predicting sepsis onset within the next 6 hours.
domain: Inpatient clinical decision support using electronic health records from a multi-hospital health system
available_data: The data would plausibly come from ICU and general ward EHR records spanning multiple years, including vitals, labs, medications, diagnoses, and timestamped nursing/clinician notes. Labels can be constructed retrospectively from chart-reviewed or consensus sepsis definitions using observed onset times, with tens of thousands of admissions and a highly imbalanced positive class.
success_metric_context: Clinicians care about early, reliable warning before physiologic deterioration, not just retrospective discrimination. Better performance means fewer missed sepsis cases at a fixed alert burden, ideally improving sensitivity at clinically acceptable specificity and lead time.
design_challenges: The task is temporally sensitive: features must be restricted to information available before the prediction window, and evaluation should use chronological splits to avoid leakage from future documentation or post-onset treatment artifacts.

Free-text notes may encode both useful early warning signals and confounding documentation patterns that change after suspicion of sepsis, so the experiment must separate genuine predictive content from label leakage and note timing effects.
known_confounds: Any observed gain could be driven by richer documentation intensity or later-note leakage rather than clinically meaningful signal, so a careful critic would check whether the text model still helps when notes are truncated to pre-alert timestamps and patient acuity is balanced.

hypothesis: A transformer-based code-risk model that combines source code, dependency metadata, and historical vulnerability fix patterns will predict high-risk open-source packages better than a model using source code alone, as measured by future confirmed CVE assignment in held-out time periods.
domain: Cybersecurity — open-source software supply-chain vulnerability risk scoring for public package registries such as npm or PyPI
available_data: Public package registry metadata, repository source code snapshots, dependency graphs, commit histories, and vulnerability disclosure records would realistically exist for this setting. A plausible dataset could span tens of thousands of packages over several years, with labels derived from packages later assigned CVEs or included in security advisories, and negative examples from packages with no known vulnerabilities in the observation window.
success_metric_context: Security teams care about identifying packages likely to receive a vulnerability disclosure before those packages are widely adopted. 'Better' means higher precision at a fixed alert budget, or higher PR-AUC under severe class imbalance, because false positives create alert fatigue while missed high-risk packages can propagate downstream harm.
design_challenges: The data are temporally structured, so evaluation must use chronological splits to avoid leakage from future disclosures or patches into training.

Positive labels are noisy and delayed because many vulnerable packages are never disclosed or are discovered long after the vulnerable code was present, creating censoring and label incompleteness that complicate fair comparison.
known_confounds: Any observed lift could be driven by correlates of package popularity or maintainer activity rather than true vulnerability risk, so a careful critic would check whether the model simply learns exposure, size, or update frequency as proxies.

hypothesis: For credit card fraud detection, a sequence model trained on raw transaction histories and recent behavioral context will detect fraudulent transactions with higher precision at the same recall than a gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated account-level engineered features.
domain: Finance — real-time card-not-present fraud screening for consumer credit/debit transactions
available_data: A payment processor or issuer would typically have millions of historical transactions over 12-24 months, each with transaction amount, merchant category, timestamp, device or channel signals, and a fraud chargeback label that arrives with delay. Account histories can be constructed from prior transactions, while engineered baselines often use rolling counts, velocity features, and spending summaries.
success_metric_context: The operational goal is to stop fraudulent transactions while minimizing false declines for legitimate customers. In practice, stakeholders care most about precision at a fixed high recall or cost-weighted savings, because unnecessary blocks directly hurt customer experience and revenue.
design_challenges: Transaction data are temporally ordered and labels are delayed, so the evaluation split must be chronological to avoid leakage from future behavior into training.
Fraud is highly imbalanced and adversarially changing over time, so the comparison must account for concept drift and may require evaluating on a later holdout period with stable threshold selection.
known_confounds: Any performance gain could come from the sequence model using more recent information or richer feature coverage rather than the modeling approach itself, so a critic would check whether both models receive identical information windows and preprocessing.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model that uses full transaction sequences and merchant-category embeddings will predict 90-day credit card charge-off risk better than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline trained on monthly aggregated customer features, when both are evaluated on a strictly future out-of-time holdout.
domain: Consumer credit risk modeling for a retail bank’s card portfolio
available_data: The bank would have historical card transaction logs, account balances, payment histories, credit bureau snapshots, and eventual delinquency/charge-off labels for millions of accounts over several years. Labels are naturally available from operational outcomes, with each account’s 90-day charge-off status determined after an observation window, and the data would include changing macroeconomic conditions and policy periods.
success_metric_context: The business goal is to identify high-risk accounts earlier enough to intervene with limit management, collections outreach, or pricing adjustments while minimizing unnecessary customer friction. In practice, better means higher out-of-time discrimination and calibration at the top of the risk list, because only a small fraction of accounts can be acted on.
design_challenges: The outcome is time-dependent and subject to leakage unless the feature window, prediction point, and label horizon are defined carefully with chronological splits.

Severe class imbalance and policy-driven label censoring can distort comparison, so the experiment must account for accounts that close, refinance, or are proactively managed before the outcome window ends.
known_confounds: Any improvement could come from the sequence model capturing recency effects or from differences in how missingness, account tenure, or macroeconomic regime shifts are represented rather than from true sequence modeling advantage.

hypothesis: A transformer model that jointly encodes product text, image embeddings, and recent click/add-to-cart sequences will outperform a text-only baseline for cold-start product recommendation on a fashion e-commerce marketplace, as measured by next-session click-through ranking quality.
domain: Fashion e-commerce marketplace product recommendation with cold-start inventory and session-level browsing behavior
available_data: A large marketplace would realistically have catalog records with titles, descriptions, category metadata, and product images, plus anonymized user session logs containing impressions, clicks, add-to-cart events, and purchases over many months. New or sparsely interacted items are common, creating a cold-start setting where labels are implicit feedback and exposure is logged rather than fully observed.
success_metric_context: The business goal is to surface items a shopper is likely to click or engage with, especially for newly listed products that have little interaction history. Better performance means improving ranking quality and downstream engagement without relying on popularity priors that favor already-known items.
design_challenges: Evaluation must handle exposure bias and implicit feedback, since unclicked items were not necessarily bad candidates and logs only reveal what was shown to users.
The model must be assessed under a temporal split to avoid leakage from future interaction patterns and seasonal fashion trends, which can strongly affect item relevance.
known_confounds: Any observed lift could be driven by popularity or category imbalance rather than multimodal understanding, so a careful critic would compare against popularity-aware and category-stratified baselines.

hypothesis: For first-pass triage of chest radiographs in an emergency department, a vision-language model fine-tuned on the radiology report text will detect urgent findings (e.g., pneumothorax, pleural effusion, or widened mediastinum) more accurately than a CNN trained only on image pixels, as measured by AUROC and sensitivity at a fixed high-specificity operating point.
domain: Emergency radiology triage in hospital chest X-ray workflows
available_data: Hospitals commonly retain paired chest X-ray images and dictated radiology reports in PACS/RIS systems, along with downstream labels such as critical-result flags or report-derived findings. A realistic dataset would include tens of thousands of studies from several years of ED and inpatient activity, with labels available either from structured report impression sections or retrospective chart review. The data are naturally time-stamped and come from multiple scanners and reading protocols.
success_metric_context: Radiology teams care about catching urgent abnormalities early without overwhelming clinicians with false alarms. Better performance means identifying more truly critical studies at the same alert volume, improving triage speed and reducing missed emergent cases.
design_challenges: The data have strong temporal and site heterogeneity, so evaluation should use a chronological or hospital-level split rather than random partitioning to avoid leakage and overoptimistic estimates.

Label quality is non-trivial because reports may describe multiple findings with varying certainty; the experiment must decide whether to use report-derived labels, adjudicated labels, or a hierarchy of urgency categories.
known_confounds: Any observed gain could come from the model exploiting report style, scanner metadata, or prevalence differences across sites or time periods rather than learning a better visual representation of urgent pathology.

hypothesis: A sequence model trained on raw clickstream and search-session events will rank products more effectively than a model using only aggregated user and item features for next-item recommendation on an e-commerce marketplace, improving click-through and conversion on held-out sessions.
domain: E-commerce marketplace product search and recommendation
available_data: The platform would realistically have timestamped session logs with queries, clicks, add-to-cart events, purchases, dwell time, and item metadata for millions of sessions over months or years. Historical relevance labels can be derived from implicit feedback such as clicks and purchases, with labels varying by session and time period. Catalog changes, promotions, and seasonality would also be recorded in operational logs.
success_metric_context: Stakeholders care about showing the most relevant products early in a session, because that directly affects click-through rate, add-to-cart rate, and downstream revenue. Better means improving top-k ranking quality for real user sessions, not just offline predictive accuracy on static interactions.
design_challenges: The data are temporally ordered and highly session-dependent, so the evaluation must avoid leakage by using chronological splits and session-level grouping rather than random sampling.
Implicit feedback is biased by position exposure and prior ranking policy, so the experiment must account for exposure bias and distinguish true relevance gains from feedback-loop effects.
known_confounds: Observed gains could come from better exploitation of frequent-popularity patterns or a shift in item exposure rather than genuine session understanding, so a critic would check whether improvements persist under propensity-aware or counterfactually adjusted evaluation.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity alert triage, a transformer model that incorporates both the raw alert text and the preceding 24-hour sequence of host/network events will achieve higher precision-at-top-K for true incident detection than a text-only baseline trained on the same labeled alerts.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage using SIEM/EDR telemetry and analyst disposition labels
available_data: A large SOC typically retains historical alert tickets, analyst labels (true positive, benign, duplicate, informational), alert descriptions, rule IDs, entity metadata, and time-stamped host/network event logs spanning months to years. In practice, there may be tens or hundreds of thousands of alerts with sparse positive incidents, plus richer context for each alert from logs collected before and after the alert timestamp. Label availability comes from analyst workflow outcomes and post-investigation incident closure records, but the positive class is usually highly imbalanced and label delay is common.
success_metric_context: Security teams care most about surfacing the small number of highest-risk alerts that warrant immediate investigation, while reducing analyst time wasted on false positives. Better performance means higher precision among the top-ranked alerts for each day or shift, and better recall of confirmed incidents under a fixed analyst review budget.
design_challenges: The data are temporally ordered and incident response labels may arrive days later, so evaluation must avoid leakage from future events and use a strict chronological split.

Alert labels are extremely imbalanced and many alerts are duplicates or correlated within the same attack campaign, so the experiment must decide whether to group related alerts by incident/host to prevent inflated gains from near-duplicate examples.
known_confounds: Any observed improvement could be driven by access to richer metadata or higher-capacity modeling rather than the sequence context itself, so a critic would check whether the benefit persists when controlling for alert source, rule ID, and campaign-level clustering.

hypothesis: Using temporally windowed user-session embeddings from clickstream sequences will predict 7-day purchase conversion more accurately than a baseline model built from aggregated session counts and recency features for an e-commerce marketplace.
domain: E-commerce marketplace user behavior modeling for conversion prediction
available_data: The platform would realistically have event-level clickstream logs for millions of anonymous user sessions over several months, including page views, searches, add-to-cart events, dwell time, device type, referral source, and whether a purchase occurred within 7 days. Historical labels are naturally available from transaction records, while session-level training examples can be constructed by joining browsing sessions to subsequent purchase outcomes.
success_metric_context: The business goal is to identify high-intent sessions for retargeting, personalization, or sales interventions, so improvement should be measured in lift at the top of the ranked list as well as calibration for decision thresholds. A better model should reduce wasted outreach while capturing more eventual purchasers.
design_challenges: The data have strong temporal dependence and concept drift from promotions, holidays, and product assortment changes, so evaluation must respect chronology rather than use random splits.

Positive labels are likely highly imbalanced and multiple sessions from the same user may be correlated, so the experiment must address leakage and choose metrics that remain meaningful under class imbalance.
known_confounds: Any observed gain could come from the sequential model exploiting leakage via near-duplicate sessions or contemporaneous campaign signals rather than true behavioral sequence understanding, so a critic would check user-level overlap and campaign-period effects.

hypothesis: A transformer-based ranker that jointly encodes product title, search query, and recent click/purchase context will outperform a query-only BM25 baseline for e-commerce search relevance ranking, measured on held-out sessions from a live marketplace.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search ranking for consumer retail products
available_data: A large marketplace typically has logged search sessions containing queries, product impressions, clicks, add-to-carts, and purchases, along with product metadata such as titles, attributes, categories, prices, and review counts. These logs are usually available over many months or years, with implicit relevance signals derived from click/purchase behavior and occasional human relevance judgments for a subset of queries.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about whether shoppers find relevant items faster and convert more often, so improvement should be reflected in ranking metrics tied to clicked or purchased items, not just offline text similarity. Better ranking should reduce abandonment and increase downstream engagement or conversion rate for search traffic.
design_challenges: Search behavior is temporally non-stationary due to seasonality, promotions, and inventory changes, so evaluation should preserve chronology rather than randomize sessions.
Implicit feedback is biased by position and exposure, so the experiment must account for click bias and distinguish true relevance gains from simple ranking-position effects.
known_confounds: Any observed lift could be explained by better popularity priors or exposure to more in-stock items rather than genuine semantic matching between query intent and product content.

hypothesis: For an online marketplace, a pairwise learning-to-rank model that uses query-item text embeddings plus recent seller performance signals will rank relevant search results more effectively than a production keyword/BM25 baseline, as measured by next-click and purchase-based ranking metrics on held-out search sessions.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search and ranking for consumer product discovery
available_data: The platform would have query logs, product titles/descriptions, click-through events, add-to-cart events, and purchases over many months of search traffic, along with seller-level quality signals such as cancellation rate, late shipment rate, and return rate. Labels can be constructed from implicit feedback in sessions, with relevance inferred from clicks, dwell time, and downstream purchases; the dataset would be large but sparse, with heavy head-tail skew in query frequency and item exposure.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about helping shoppers find items they are likely to click and buy while avoiding low-quality listings that create bad experiences and refunds. Better means improving user satisfaction and conversion, not merely maximizing click volume on popular items.
design_challenges: Search logs are temporally non-stationary because inventory, seasonality, and promotions change over time, so evaluation needs a chronological split rather than random sampling.

Implicit feedback is biased by position exposure and popularity, making it non-trivial to distinguish true relevance gains from changes in where items are shown; the experiment must account for selection bias and exposure effects.
known_confounds: Observed improvements could come from better handling of popular queries or seller quality penalties rather than genuinely better semantic matching, so a careful critic would check performance separately on tail queries and against exposure-adjusted baselines.
